does: This paper introduces DRIP-R, a benchmark for evaluating how large language model agents handle ambiguous real-world policies in retail return scenarios. The benchmark comprises policy-ambiguous return situations paired with customer personas, a conversational simulation environment with tool-calling capabilities, and a multi-judge evaluation framework assessing policy adherence, dialogue quality, behavioral alignment, and resolution quality. Through experiments on frontier models, the authors demonstrate that these models fundamentally disagree on identical policy-ambiguous scenarios, revealing systematic challenges in LLM decision-making when facing the inherent ambiguities prevalent in real-world policy interpretation.
connects: This work addresses a significant gap in existing agent evaluation literature, which typically assumes well-specified, unambiguous policies despite real-world domains featuring inherently ambiguous guidelines admitting multiple valid interpretations. It builds on prior research in LLM-based agent evaluation and benchmarking, extending beyond existing frameworks that focus on clear-cut decision scenarios. The work connects to broader research on conversational AI, dialogue quality assessment, and tool-use in language models, while also engaging with foundational questions about how AI systems should handle underspecified requirements—a challenge recognized in AI safety and alignment research where policy specification and value disagreement are central concerns.
enables: This benchmark establishes evaluation methodology for an important class of real-world deployment scenarios where policies are inherently ambiguous. It opens research directions around building agents that gracefully handle policy ambiguity through better uncertainty quantification, clarification-seeking dialogue strategies, or principled approaches to resolving competing valid interpretations. The framework enables investigation of how to align agent behavior with human expectations in ambiguous contexts, potentially informing techniques for value learning, preference elicitation, and human-AI collaboration. Future work could extend this approach to other domains beyond retail and develop methods that not only recognize ambiguity but actively engage stakeholders in principled resolution.
